coredata.dc:title: Ordered mesoporous silica encapsulated carbon quantum dots and its application in Fe3+ detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd and Techna Group S.r.l.
coredata.dc:creator: Dong, Yufei
Ma, Jianzhong
Liu, Chao
Bao, Yan
coredata.dc:description: The nanocomposites prepared by the combination of SiO2 and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the advantages of low toxicity, high fluorescence intensity, high biocompatibility and good water solubility, which greatly broaden its application range. Nevertheless, if only SiO2 and CQDs are simply combined without any structural design, the stability of the nanocomposites will be poor, which will affect its practical application value. In this work, we first synthesized ordered mesoporous silica (OMS) rapidly in aqueous solution by a facile method (30 min), and then generated CQDs in situ in the mesoporous channels of OMS by hydrothermal method to obtain CQDs@OMS fluorescent nanocomposites. The chemical structure and morphology of OMS and CQDs@OMS were characterized by FT-IR, XRD, SAXRD and TEM, indicating that CQDs was successfully encapsulated in OMS. The stability test results of CQDs@OMS indicate that CQDs can be stably present in the mesoporous channels of OMS, and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS nanocomposites is almost independent of ionic strength, pH and storage time. The optical performance results show that the optimal excitation wavelength of CQDs@OMS nanocomposites is about 340 nm, the emission wavelength is about 440 nm. Using CQDs@OMS nanocomposites as fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+, it was found that CQDs@OMS responded very quickly to Fe3+. The fluorescence quenching intensity of CQDs@OMS has a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 25–750 μM. Therefore, the trace amount of Fe3+ in solution can be calculated by measuring the relative magnitude of fluorescence intensity.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ordered mesoporous silica
Encapsulate carbon quantum dots
Fluorescent nanocomposites
Stability
Fluorescence probe
Detection of Fe3+

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application of CQDs@OMS in Fe3+ detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The FT-IR spectra (a) of OMS and CQDs@OMS, and XRD patterns (b) of CQDs, OMS and CQDs@OMS.
The SAXRD pattern of OMS and CQDs@OMS.
TEM images of different sample: OMS (a, b), CQDs (c) and CQDs@OMS (d, e, f).
TEM image of CQDs@OMS (a) and the corresponding EDX elemental mapping images for Si (b), O (c), and C (d), respectively.

Optical photos of aqueous dispersions of different samples before (a, b) and after (c, d) standing for 48 h under fluorescent and ultraviolet lamps (samples from left to right were OMS, CQDs, CQDs/OMS and CQDs@OMS).
Effect of (a) ionic strength, (b) pH, (c) storage time on fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS.
(a) Uv–vis and fluorescence spectra of CQDs and CQDs@OMS (Excitation spectra: Ex; Emission spectra: Em); (b) Emission spectra of CQDs@OMS at different excitation wavelengths (280 nm–420 nm).
Effect of reaction time between CQDs@OMS and Fe3+ on fluorescence intensity.
Effect of Fe3+ concentration on fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS (a); Linear relationship between F0/F and Fe3+ concentration (b).
Schematic diagram of OMS formation mechanism.

head.ce:title: Ordered mesoporous silica encapsulated carbon quantum dots and its application in Fe3+ detection

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi'an, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shaanxi Collaborative Innovation Center of Industrial Auxiliary Chemistry and Technology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering, Shaanxi University of Science & Technology, Xi'an, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Bioresources Chemical and Materials Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nanocomposites prepared by the combination of SiO2 and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have the advantages of low toxicity, high fluorescence intensity, high biocompatibility and good water solubility, which greatly broaden its application range. Nevertheless, if only SiO2 and CQDs are simply combined without any structural design, the stability of the nanocomposites will be poor, which will affect its practical application value. In this work, we first synthesized ordered mesoporous silica (OMS) rapidly in aqueous solution by a facile method (30 min), and then generated CQDs in situ in the mesoporous channels of OMS by hydrothermal method to obtain CQDs@OMS fluorescent nanocomposites. The chemical structure and morphology of OMS and CQDs@OMS were characterized by FT-IR, XRD, SAXRD and TEM, indicating that CQDs was successfully encapsulated in OMS. The stability test results of CQDs@OMS indicate that CQDs can be stably present in the mesoporous channels of OMS, and the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS nanocomposites is almost independent of ionic strength, pH and storage time. The optical performance results show that the optimal excitation wavelength of CQDs@OMS nanocomposites is about 340 nm, the emission wavelength is about 440 nm. Using CQDs@OMS nanocomposites as fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+, it was found that CQDs@OMS responded very quickly to Fe3+. The fluorescence quenching intensity of CQDs@OMS has a good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 25–750 μM. Therefore, the trace amount of Fe3+ in solution can be calculated by measuring the relative magnitude of fluorescence intensity.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new type of carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterials, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) not only has the fluorescence performance comparable to traditional QDs, but also has excellent properties incomparable by QDs, such as ease of production, good water solubility and chemical stability, excellent biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and so on [1–8]. Therefore, CQDs have attracted widespread research interest once discovered. It has made significant contributions to the development of biological labels, bioimaging, drug delivery, electrochemistry and optical detection [9–17]. Nevertheless, it has been reported that CQDs with their extremely small size and excellent water solubility enable them to through the cell wall (membrane), and combine with the nucleus. This phenomenon makes it possible to have potential effects on the health of organisms [18]. Therefore, it is worth studying how to ensure its own performance and maintain its stability in the application process.

Ordered mesoporous silica (OMS) has large specific surface area and pore volume, tunable pore size, low toxicity, high biocompatibility and good chemical stability [19–21]. The long-range, uniform and ordered pore structure make it suitable for encapsulating small-size nanomaterials. Encapsulation of CQDs in the channels of the OMS can not only make the CQDs stable, but also not interrupt the excitation and emission of CQDs due to the silica is considered to be optically transparent [22]. Consequently, the prepared nanocomposites have high fluorescence intensity, good biocompatibility and chemical stability, and low cytotoxicity due to the advantages of both. So, it is expected to be well applied in biomedicine and optical detection. Wang et al. [23] prepared the carbon dots (CDs) in situ and introduced it into the amino-functionalized ordered mesoporous silica (SBA-NH2) through a simple microplasma-assisted method. The CDs/SBA-NH2 nanocomposites preserved the high specific surface area of mesoporous silica and the optical properties of CDs. Compared with SBA-NH2, the CDs/SBA-NH2 nanocomposites have high efficiency of uranium adsorption and can simultaneously monitor the adsorption process in situ. This work has provided a new strategy for in situ monitoring of the adsorption process of uranium and other metal ions. Xu et al. [24] used periodic mesoporous silica as the imprinting matrix, and amino-CDs as the “functional monomer” directly to prepare mesoporous structured molecularly imprinted polymer capped carbon dots (M-MIPs@CDs) fluorescence sensor by immobilizing amino-CDs into mesoporous structured imprinted polymer, which was used for highly sensitive and selective determination of TNT. The method proposed in this work was proved to be a convenient and practical method to prepare highly sensitivity and selective fluorescence MIPs@CDs sensors. However, the preparation of OMS usually requires hydrothermal method or sol-gel method, which have their own disadvantages, such as long reaction time, organic solvents in the reaction process and so on. Therefore, exploring a synthesis method that saves time, energy and environmentally friendly is not only beneficial to the practical production and application of OMS, but also the efficient preparation of OMS-CQDs nanocomposites.

In this work, OMS was synthesized rapidly (30 min) in aqueous solution via a facile method. Carbon quantum dots were generated in situ in the channels of OMS by hydrothermal method to obtain CQDs@OMS nanocomposites. The morphology, structure and fluorescence properties of the prepared CQDs@OMS were explored and used to detect Fe3+. It was found that the quenching effect of Fe3+ on CQDs@OMS was obvious, and the concentration of Fe3+ showed a good linear relationship with the fluorescence quenching intensity of CQDs@OMS within a certain range. So, CQDs@OMS provides a foundation for the visualization technology of it to detect Fe3+. Meanwhile, CQDs@OMS has good biocompatibility and almost no toxicity, so it is expected to be applied to detect the content of Fe3+ in blood.

Cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) and Tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) were purchased from Ke Miou chemical reagent co Ltd. Sodium hydroxide, Citric acid and Ethylenediamine were obtained from Hongyan reagent factory. Iron nitrate nonahydrate (Fe(NO3)3·9H2O) were received from Damao chemical reagent factory. The deionized water was from our laboratory. All chemicals and reagents were of analytical standards and used as-received without further purification.

0.160 g of cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB) was dissolved in 70 g of deionized water, then 0.5 mL of NaOH (2 mol/L) solution was added, and the mixture was stirred at 300 r/min for 15 min at 80 °C. After adding 1 mL of tetraethylorthosilicate (TEOS), the solution changed from transparent to white after a few minutes. Stirring was continued for 15 min and the reaction was completed. The product was centrifuged and washed with deionized water and dried in an oven. Finally, the surfactant was removed by calcining at 550 °C for 3 h in an air atmosphere to obtain ordered mesoporous silica.

Some OMS microspheres were dispersed uniformly in 80 mL aqueous solution by ultrasonication. Then 3 g of citric acid and 1 mL of ethylenediamine were added and stirred until completely dissolved. The mixed solution was hydrothermally reacted at 220 °C for 5 h, raw material mixture of CQDs was fed into the mesoporous channels of OMS under high temperature and high pressure conditions, and CQDs was generated in situ in the channels. After centrifugation, the precipitate was washed thoroughly and dried in an oven at 60 °C to produce the CQDs@OMS nanocomposites.

Detection of Fe3+ was performed at room temperature. CQDs@OMS was dispersed in a certain amount of deionized water and configured to a dispersion solution with a concentration of 0.5 mg/mL. Transfered 2 mL of CQDs@OMS dispersion into the cuvette, added appropriate amount of Fe(NO3)3 solution. The concentration of Fe3+ in the CQDs@OMS dispersion was 25–750 μmol/L. After standing for 30 s, the fluorescence intensity F of the system was measured at the excitation wavelength of 340 nm. Similarly, the fluorescence intensity of the blank reagent without Fe3+ was determined as F0 by the same method.

Fluorescence visualization: Fe3+ solution with a concentration of 750 μmol/L was added to the CQDs@OMS dispersion and placed under ultraviolet lamp at 365 nm to observe the quenching of its fluorescence intensity.

The average particle size was measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS) using Malvern Zeta-sizer Nano-ZS 90 (Malvern Instruments Ltd., UK) instrument with 90° back scattering measurements at 25 °C. The microscopic morphology and EDX of all the samples were observed using Tecnai G2 F20 S-TWIN transmission electron microscopy (TEM) manufactured by FEI Corporation of the United States. FT-IR spectra of all the samples were recorded on Vertex70 (Bruker, Germany) spectrophotometer using KBr disks. Wide-angle XRD was performed on a D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer from Bruker, Germany. Ultraviolet spectroscopy was performed using Carry 5000 spectrophotometer manufactured by Agilent in the United States. The fluorescence of samples were tested at room temperature using a FS5 fluorescence spectrometer manufactured by Edinburgh, UK.

The current research suggested that the formation of OMS depends on the micelles formed by the surfactant in solution as a template, and accelerated the hydrolysis rate of the silicon source by alkaline conditions, so that the silicon source was hydrolyzed and grown along the surfactant micelle template to form OMS. The final morphology of the OMS was controlled by the morphology of the surfactant micelle template [25–27]. However, in this study, we found that alkaline conditions could not only accelerate the hydrolysis rate of the silicon source, but also affect the template formed by the surfactant in the solution. Which would ultimately affect the morphology of the generated silica. Therefore, this theory was validated by characterizing the template formed by surfactant and the final silica morphology in neutral and alkaline reaction systems, respectively.

It can be observed from Fig. S1, the dissolving state and the size of the template formed by surfactant were different in water when the pH of the system was 7 and 10, respectively. After adding the silicon source, OMS can be obtained after reaction in the system with a pH of 10 for 15 min. But only a small amount of silica microspheres prepared after 4 h of reaction in a system of pH = 7, and the rest were small particles generated by hydrolysis of silicon source (Fig. S2). These results confirmed that the alkaline conditions can not only accelerate the hydrolysis rate of silicon source, but also affect the template formed by the surfactant in solution, and ultimately affect the morphology of the generated silica.

Based on the above results, the formation mechanism of OMS was proposed. As shown in Scheme 1 , first of all, CTAB formed a single molecule in aqueous solution (a). After adding NaOH, its concentration in the alkaline solution was greater than its critical micelle concentration, thus formed micelle with hydrophobic chain inwards and hydrophilic head outwards (b). Under the action of rapid stirring, the micelles are further assembled to form a rod-like shape with the extension of time (c), and finally formed spherical micelles (d). After adding silicon source, the template action of the CTAB micelles was greatly exerted. The silicon source was negatively charged due to the adsorption of OH- in the solution, and the positively charged micelle formed by the surfactant was deposited by electrostatic force and van der Waals force, enabled the silicon source can be precipitated in the organic network of the CTAB micelles, and the two polymerized and solidified to form the pore wall (e). After the CTAB template was removed, ordered channels are obtained in silica (f).

The chemical structure of the synthesized samples was characterized by FT-IR spectrum. It can be observed in Fig. 1 a that the strong and wide absorption band at about 1080 cm−1, which is attributed to the antisymmetric stretching vibration absorption of Si–O–Si [28]. The peak at 967 cm−1 is caused by the bending vibration absorption of Si–OH. The peak near 1625 cm−1 is the H–O–H bending vibration peak of water. And the peaks at 806 cm−1 and 461 cm−1 are belonging to the symmetric stretching vibration absorption of Si–O [29–32]. The result of the FT-IR analysis confirms that the OMS is silica. After CQDs are encapsulated in silica, the FT-IR spectrum of the OMS still has the characteristic peaks of silica. In addition, there are obvious absorption peaks at 2926 cm−1 and 2855 cm−1, corresponding to the asymmetric stretching vibrations of –CH3 and –CH2, respectively. The peaks at 1659 cm−1 and 1381 cm−1 are the stretching vibration peak of CO and the deformation vibration peak of O–H in the carboxyl group, respectively. And the peak at 1234 cm−1 is the stretching vibration absorption peak of C–O–C [33–35]. It is known that the surface of the sample acquired after silica encapsulation of CQDs contain a large amount of hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and other oxygen-containing groups, which prove that the sample have strong hydrophilicity [36].

Fig. 1b demonstrated that the prepared silica nanoparticles have no sharp diffraction peaks on the X-ray diffraction spectrum, but a broad diffraction package between 14° and 36°. It's the typical steamed bread peak demonstrates that the silica sample is amorphous state [37]. CQDs showed a strong peak centered at 23.7° along with a shoulder centered at 41.7°. These peaks are assigned to [100], [101] planes presenting a higher number of oxygen-containing groups [38–41]. The EDX analysis results indicated that CQDs might be N-doped (Fig. S3). After CQDs are encapsulated in silica, the peaks of SiO2 at 22.5° and CQDs at 41.7° did not change significantly in the XRD pattern. The peak of CQDs at 23.7° is shifted to 28.9°. According to the Bragg equation2dsinθ = λ (λ is the incident wavelength and remains unchanged), θ increases, indicating that the crystal plane distance d decreases. This may be due to the CQDs encapsulated in the OMS channels are limited by the size of the mesopores, resulting in the unit cell to shrink. Combined with FT-IR spectrum and XRD pattern, it can be seen that there are silica and CQDs in the sample.

Small-angle X-ray diffraction is usually used to judge the existence of ordered mesoporous structure, and the appearance of diffraction peaks is the basis for confirming the existence of ordered mesoporous structure [42–44]. It can be seen from the SAXRD pattern (Fig. 2 ) of silica that a strong diffraction peak appeared at 2θ = 2.41°, and the other two weak diffraction peaks were located at 2θ = 4.13° and 2θ = 4.74°, respectively. The corresponding diffraction peaks represent crystal planes [100], [110] and [200], respectively [45–47]. These results indicate that the prepared silica has highly and long-range order mesoporous structure. When CQDs are encapsulated in OMS, the peaks at 2θ = 2.41°, 4.13° and 4.74° disappear, which suggest that the highly ordered mesoporous structure of the OMS disappear. This may be attributed to the widespread distribution of CQDs in the ordered mesoporous channels of OMS, resulting in the destruction of the order degree of the mesoporous. This also proves that CQDs have been successfully encapsulated in the channels of OMS.

To further confirm that the OMS possesses apparent ordered mesoporous structure, the samples were observed by TEM. As shown in the enlarged of Fig. 3 b, the typical TEM images of OMS clearly reveal that all those samples are ordered hexagonal mesoporous in character. Channels both perpendicular and parallel to the particle surface can be observed, this is consistent with the micromorphology of OMS reported in the literature [48]. The diameter distribution of OMS microspheres acquired is about 90–110 nm, and the pore size of ordered mesoporous pore is about 3–3.5 nm. It can be observed from Fig. 3c, the CQDs is spherical, with uniform particle size, good dispersion and no aggregation. The particle size is about 2 nm, which is completely smaller than the mesoporous pore size of OMS (3–3.5 nm). It can be directly observed from Fig. 3d that when CQDs are encapsulated in the mesoporous pores of the OMS, the color of the part filled by CQDs is obviously deepened, and most of the long-range ordered mesoporous structure of OMS become disordered structure. This phenomenon is consistent with the SAXRD test results. It is more obvious than the results reported in the literature [23]. As can be seen from Fig. 3e and f, there are also some CQDs that are uniformly bonded to the surface of the OMS through electrostatic interaction. The CQDs@OMS was also analysed using TEM coupled with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) to confirm the composition of CQDs@OMS [49]. Fig. 4 showed the EDX elemental mapping of Si, O, and C, respectively. A large number of C elements are consistent with the distribution of Si and O elements, which further confirm the composition and structure of CQDs@OMS.

Because CQDs have a good water solubility, it may be dissolved from the channels of OMS in the aqueous solution. Consequently, the stability of CQDs@OMS was investigated. As revealed in Fig. 5 , the aqueous dispersions of OMS, CQDs, CQDs/OMS, and CQDs@OMS (CQDs/OMS means that the ultrasonic dispersions of OMS in the aqueous dispersion of CQDs) are arranged from left to right in each picture. As can be seen from Figs. 5a and 4b before standing that when all the samples are dispersed in aqueous solution by ultrasound, a uniform aqueous dispersion can be formed. Under the ultraviolet light with a wavelength of 365 nm, all samples except the OMS show fluorescence (CQDs and CQDs/OMS manifest blue fluorescence, while CQDs@OMS manifest blue-violet fluorescence).

However, after standing for 48 h (c, d), in the aqueous dispersions of OMS and CQDs/OMS, the OMS is all precipitated at the bottom. This may be due to the OMS itself is dispersed in aqueous solution and gradually precipitate at the bottom with the extension of time. And CQDs/OMS simply mixes CQDs and OMS together without any interaction between them, so OMS will precipitate at the bottom, while the CQDs could be dispersed stably in aqueous solution due to extremely small particle size and excellent hydrophilicity. However, the aqueous dispersion of CQDs@OMS does not precipitate before and after standing. This is due to the surface of CQDs contains a large amount of hydroxyl groups, carboxyl groups and other oxygen-containing groups, which makes it highly hydrophilic. It's distributed in the mesoporous channels of OMS, which makes the CQDs@OMS nanocomposites have excellent hydrophilicity and thus be well dispersed in aqueous solution. After standing for 48 h, there is no precipitation and the fluorescence color and intensity remained almost unchanged under the ultraviolet light. This also indicates that CQDs doesn't dissolve from the mesoporous channels of OMS, but exist stably in the mesoporous pores.

In order to further evaluate the stability of CQDs@OMS in different environments, the effects of ionic strength, pH and storage time on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS were investigated. Fig. 6 a showed the stability of CQDs@OMS in different concentrations of sodium chloride solution. When the ionic strength in the solution is changed, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS doesn't decrease significantly with the increase of the concentration of sodium chloride solution (up to 0.5 mmol/L). This indicates that the ionic strength has no obvious influence on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS to some extent and CQDs@OMS has excellent colloidal stability [50,51]. Fig. 6b showed the change of fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS within the pH range of 2–12. When the pH changes from 2 to 8, the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS increased slowly with the increase of pH, and reached the maximum at pH = 8, then slightly decreased with the increase of pH. This is due to under acidic conditions, functional groups such as carbonyl and carboxyl on the surface of CQDs@OMS formed hydrogen bond, which was prone to aggregation and thus lead to weakened fluorescence intensity. In a strongly alkaline environment, the formed carboxylate destroyed the surface passivation of CQDs@OMS, thus quenched the fluorescence. The storage stability of CQDs@OMS was tested at room temperature for 7 weeks. The results demonstrated that its fluorescence intensity was basically unchanged (Fig. 6c), and no precipitation or suspension formation was observed. This phenomenon further indicated that CQDs@OMS had good storage stability and was conducive to practical application [52].

The UV–visible absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra of CQDs and CQDs@OMS are shown in Fig. 7 a. It can be seen from the UV–Vis spectra that CQDs and CQDs@OMS possess significant absorption in the ultraviolet region (200–400 nm), and the maximum absorption peak is around 340 nm, which is due to the n-π* transition of CO and π-π* transition of conjugate CC [53–55]. Compared with CQDs, the absorption peak intensity of CQDs@OMS is weaker than that of CQDs. It can be seen from the fluorescence spectra that the excitation and emission wavelengths of CQDs and CQDs@OMS are consistent. The optimal excitation wavelength is about 340 nm and the emission wavelength is about 440 nm. But the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS is lower than that of CQDs, which express the same trend with the ultraviolet absorption intensity. This may be ascribed at the same concentration (c = 0.5 mg/mL), the amount of CQDs in CQDs@OMS is less than that of pure CQDs, so its ultraviolet absorption intensity and fluorescence intensity are lower than that of pure CQDs.

In addition, the emission spectra and the changes in fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS at different excitation wavelengths were also investigated. In the experiment, the emission spectra of CQDs@OMS were tested by changing the excitation wavelengths from 280 nm to 420 nm. As shown in Fig. 7b, we can clearly observe that the fluorescence emission intensity increase at first and then decrease with the increase of excitation wavelength. When the excitation wavelength is 340 nm, the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs@OMS is the strongest, which is consistent with the test results in Fig. 8 a. However, when the excitation wavelength changes from 280 nm to 420 nm, the emission wavelength of CQDs@OMS shift from 440 nm to 490 nm with the increase of excitation wavelength. This indicates that CQDs@OMS has excitation-dependent performance. This is owing to the surface of CQDs existing in CQDs@OMS has the similar properties of quantum dots, such as size effect, point defect, edge effect, etc., causing the maximum emission peak redshift [56,57].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CQDs@OMS in Fe3+ detection
ce:section.ce:para: 3.5.1. Response time of CQDs@OMS to Fe3+. The presence of metal ions may affect the fluorescence properties of CQDs. We investigated a large number of literature and selected different metal ions to assess their fluorescence performance of CQDs under the same conditions [58]. The results indicated the remarkable selectivity of the CQDs for Fe3+ (Fig. S4). The effect of reaction time between CQDs@OMS and Fe3+ on fluorescence intensity was investigated to evaluate the response time of CQDs@OMS to Fe3+. It can be seen from Fig. 8 that the fluorescence intensity of the original aqueous solution of CQDs@OMS is relatively high, but the fluorescence intensity decreased sharply after Fe3+ is added, and the fluorescence intensity stabilized after 30s. It indicates that CQDs@OMS responds very quickly to Fe3+. Therefore, a method for rapid detection of Fe3+ can be established.

3.5.2. Response curve of CQDs@OMS to Fe3+. The quenching degree of Fe3+ on the fluorescence intensity of CQDs@OMS was investigated by measuring the fluorescence emission intensity under excitation at 340 nm for different Fe3+ concentrations. As shown in Fig. 9 a, the fluorescence emission intensity of CQDs@OMS (c = 0.5 mg/mL) decreases continuously with increasing Fe3+ concentration. This may be attributed to Fe3+ can complex with the hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of CQDs in fluorescent CQDs@OMS, resulting in the electrons in excited states transfer from the surface of CQDs in fluorescent CQDs@OMS to the half-filled 3d orbital of the Fe3+ions and quenches the fluorescence of CQDs@OMS [59,60]. The concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 25–750 μM has a good linear relationship with the fluorescence quenching intensity of CQDs@OMS (Fig. 9b) [61]. When the concentration of Fe3+ is 750 μM, the quenching rate of fluorescence intensity is 95%. The linear regression equation is F0/F = 0.02666C-0.42599, and the linear correlation coefficient R [2] = 0.996. Therefore, we can calculate the trace amount of Fe3+ in solution by measuring the relative magnitude of fluorescence intensity. The illustration in Fig. 9b is an ultraviolet fluorescence photograph of Fe3+ quenched CQDs@OMS. It can be clearly observed that CQDs@OMS shows bright blue-violet color under the ultraviolet light of 365 nm (as shown on the left). While after adding Fe3+ with the concentration of 750 μM, the quenching effect is extremely obvious and the fluorescence almost disappeared (as shown on the right). Consequently, the foundation for the visualization technology of CQDs@OMS to detect Fe3+ can be established.

In summary, OMS was rapidly prepared using the surfactant CTAB as template under alkaline conditions. This method reduced the preparation time of OMS from a few hours to 30 min, and no organic solvent was used. Afterwards, CQDs were generated in situ in the mesoporous channels of OMS by hydrothermal method to prepare CQDs@OMS fluorescent nanocomposites. CQDs can be stably present in the mesoporous channels of OMS, and CQDs@OMS has good fluorescence stability under different environments. Using CQDs@OMS nanocomposites as fluorescent probes to detect Fe3+, the fluorescence quenching effect of Fe3+ on CQDs@OMS was very obvious. The concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 25–750 μM showed a good linear relationship with the fluorescence quenching intensity of CQDs@OMS. The linear regression equation is F0/F = 0.02666C-0.42599, and the linear correlation coefficient R [2] = 0.996. When the concentration of Fe3+ was 750 μM, the quenching rate of the fluorescence intensity was up to 95%. This not only establishes the foundation for the visualization technology of CQDs@OMS to detect Fe3+, but also calculates the trace amount of Fe3+ in solution by measuring the relative magnitude of fluorescence intensity. Meanwhile, CQDs@OMS has good biocompatibility and almost no toxicity, so it is expected to be applied to detect the content of Fe3+ in organisms and blood as fluorescent probes.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFB0308602), the Natural Science Basic Research Plan in Shaanxi Province of China (2018JQ5211) and the Special Scientific Research Program Founded by Shaanxi Provincial Education Department (18JK0102).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is/are the supplementary data to this article:
Application Application

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ceramint.2020.01.131.
